question_text: Which statement best describes RN case management in M01?
answer_a: It applies registered nursing judgment to patient-care risks, coordination needs, advocacy, and follow-up evidence.
answer_b: It is limited to appointment scheduling and referral tracking.
answer_c: It allows the RN to independently diagnose new medical conditions during coordination.
answer_d: It is primarily a business process for guaranteeing service approval.
rationale: RN case management remains nursing practice when actions are tied to assessment, patient risk, scope, coordination, and documented follow-up. The other answers remove the nursing judgment link or exceed RN authority.

question_text: A referral packet lists a new medication and the caregiver reports the patient is confused about the instructions. What is the best first RN case management response?
answer_a: Tell the caregiver to stop the medication until the next appointment.
answer_b: Assess what the caregiver understands, compare it with available discharge information, and identify whether provider clarification is needed.
answer_c: Promise the family that additional visits will be approved to solve the concern.
answer_d: Document that the caregiver is noncompliant and close the referral.
rationale: The RN uses assessment before planning or coordination. Medication confusion requires information gathering, scope-safe teaching from existing instructions, possible escalation, and factual documentation.

question_text: Which example is indirect patient care within RN case management?
answer_a: Filing a fax cover sheet without reviewing why the information matters.
answer_b: Advertising certificate release before provider metadata is available.
answer_c: Calling a home health agency to confirm receipt of a wound-care referral because delayed care could increase infection risk.
answer_d: Changing a wound-care order after the supply company reports a delay.
rationale: Referral follow-up can be indirect patient care when it is connected to a patient-care risk such as delayed wound care. Changing orders is outside independent RN authority.

question_text: A family asks the RN case manager to increase a diuretic dose because the patient gained weight after discharge. Which response best protects scope?
answer_a: Agree to the dose increase because the symptom appears cardiac.
answer_b: Suggest skipping the medication until the family feels comfortable.
answer_c: Tell the family that medication decisions are not important to case management.
answer_d: Assess reported symptoms, reinforce existing urgent instructions within scope, and escalate medication concerns to the provider pathway.
rationale: The RN may assess, reinforce existing instructions, document, and escalate. Independently changing or stopping medication would be a provider-only decision and unsafe.

question_text: Which goal is most patient-centered and useful for RN case management follow-up?
answer_a: Patient and caregiver will state two discharge warning signs and know whom to contact before the next follow-up call.
answer_b: The payer will approve all requested services by the end of the day.
answer_c: The RN will complete all internal tasks without documenting patient response.
answer_d: The family will receive whatever services they prefer.
rationale: The correct goal is measurable, tied to patient understanding and safety, and within RN coordination scope. The other options promise outcomes or focus on tasks without patient-care evaluation.

question_text: During a follow-up call, a patient reports severe shortness of breath and chest pressure. What should the RN case manager do?
answer_a: Leave a routine message for the provider to review next week.
answer_b: Follow emergency or urgent escalation policy and document the reported symptoms, instructions given, notifications, and follow-up plan.
answer_c: Treat the report as a scheduling concern because case managers do not address symptoms.
answer_d: Ask the patient to wait until the assignment activity is reviewed.
rationale: Severe shortness of breath and chest pressure may indicate immediate risk. The RN should use the urgent pathway, stay within policy, and document symptoms, action, and follow-up.

question_text: Which note excerpt is most audit-ready for M01 coordination evidence?
answer_a: Handled patient issue and called team.
answer_b: Family upset, told them everything should be fine.
answer_c: Caregiver reports two missed doses due to label confusion; RN reviewed existing discharge instructions, requested provider clarification, and will confirm response by 1500.
answer_d: Approved more nursing visits because patient wanted help.
rationale: The correct note identifies the source, patient-care issue, RN action, escalation, and follow-up time frame. The other notes are vague or claim authority outside RN scope.

question_text: A caregiver says transportation barriers may cause the patient to miss a high-risk follow-up visit. Which action best reflects RN advocacy?
answer_a: Guarantee that transportation will be provided by the payer.
answer_b: Ignore the concern because transportation is not clinical.
answer_c: Tell the caregiver the patient is responsible and close the case.
answer_d: Document the barrier, explain the patient-care risk, coordinate appropriate resources or referrals, and escalate unresolved risk as needed.
rationale: Advocacy means raising barriers that affect safety or continuity and coordinating appropriate help without promising outcomes outside RN authority.

question_text: Which action is most clearly direct patient care by the RN case manager?
answer_a: Assessing a patient's understanding of discharge warning signs during a follow-up call and documenting teach-back.
answer_c: Uploading a certificate template before approval metadata is available.
answer_d: Renaming a referral file without reviewing patient-care content.
rationale: Assessing understanding and documenting teach-back involve direct interaction, nursing assessment, education within scope, and evaluation of patient response.

question_text: A staff member asks whether sending paperwork is RN case management or administrative support. Which answer best applies M01 role sorting?
answer_a: It is always RN case management because all paperwork affects care.
answer_b: It depends on whether RN judgment is needed to select, explain, or act on clinical information tied to patient-care risk.
answer_c: It is always provider-only because paperwork can contain medical facts.
answer_d: It should never be documented because it is not face-to-face care.
rationale: Borderline tasks are classified by patient-care relevance and required judgment. Pure transmission may be support work, while clinically selecting and acting on information can be RN case management.

question_text: Which statement correctly describes M01 completion and certificate release?
answer_a: Completing M01 automatically releases a certificate.
answer_b: A certificate can be released once the learner views the overview page.
answer_c: M01 completion evidence is required, and certificate release remains blocked until provider approval metadata and course completion gates are satisfied.
answer_d: Certificate release is unrelated to Moodle evidence.
rationale: M01 completion contributes evidence, but certificate release requires course-level completion evidence and provider approval metadata. The draft module cannot independently unlock a certificate.

question_text: In a fictional heart failure follow-up scenario, which RN action is unsafe?
answer_a: Clarifying the caregiver's report of weight gain and shortness of breath.
answer_b: Documenting that provider notification is pending and follow-up is planned.
answer_c: Explaining existing discharge warning signs within nursing scope.
answer_d: Independently ordering an increased diuretic dose to prevent readmission.
rationale: Independently changing medication therapy is outside RN case management scope. The RN should assess, educate within existing instructions, document, and escalate to the provider pathway.

question_text: Which learner-facing status language is appropriate for M01?
answer_a: M01 is a 3 contact-hour draft module pending BRN CEP approval, with no approval claim or certificate release until required gates are satisfied.
answer_b: M01 can omit status language because it is only a build file.
answer_c: M01 should be labeled primarily by a different measurement instead of contact hours.
answer_d: M01 changes its measurement whenever expanded content is added.
rationale: The module target is 3 contact hours and status remains Draft / Pending BRN CEP Approval. Expanded content does not change the target or create an approval claim.

question_text: A referral delay may leave a patient without ordered wound supplies for several days. Which documentation best supports RN case management?
answer_a: Supply issue noted; no further details needed.
answer_b: Ordered wound supplies unavailable; RN identified infection-risk concern, contacted supplier and ordering pathway according to policy, notified supervisor of delay risk, and set follow-up time.
answer_c: Patient probably did not plan well, so case closed.
answer_d: RN promised delivery today to reassure the patient.
rationale: The correct documentation links the supply delay to patient-care risk, shows coordination and escalation, avoids blame, and identifies follow-up evidence.

question_text: Why is evaluation essential in RN case management documentation?
answer_a: It allows the RN to avoid documenting pending issues.
answer_b: It replaces the need for provider communication.
answer_c: It shows whether coordination addressed the patient-care risk, whether understanding or response was confirmed, and what follow-up remains.
answer_d: It is only needed for direct hands-on care.
rationale: Evaluation closes the coordination loop by showing outcomes, remaining barriers, patient or caregiver response, and next steps. Without evaluation, the record may show only an attempted task.
